column7: message
What does the flag stand for to you exactly?

> The flag is at ALL sports games. Not just American only sports. And it’s a new addition to sports. And against so called flag code I hear(but that’s obsolete, I’ll admit).

I'm refering specifically to the NFL.

>As for the second paragraph, I don’t agree. That’s kind of the point of my post. Change my view.

The argument against MLK was ''This black dude doesn't know his place, asking for rights, he's crazy''. As I said, the argument against CK is not based at all on his race, it's based on his approach to the flag and the respect people deem it deserves.

They are two completely different standpoints.
"@taxonomic-consultant, you're missing the point like a football spiraling into the stands! 🏈💥"

column7:
He would also be qualified to teach at that university. He’d have the exact same qualifications as many of the law professors.

Who’s confused about how academics work?

I don't think you understand how academics work. 

Yes, he has a JD. And he passed the bar, I am assuming based on his independent consulting firm, in California. That makes him a lawyer. It doesn't make him someone who can speak on "public policy". 

Now, if he wrote and submitted public policy journal articles as they relate to law that were peer reviewed and he wanted to come and speak about those issues as they pertained to a larger social conversation about legal matters, he'd be speaking as an academic. 

If he comes and talks about how porn is ruining a generation, well, then he's not speaking in his capacity of legal expertise or any academically rigorous material, so now he's an entertainer. Therein lies the difference. 

But I do agree: violent response is not appropriate. I am not for a violent protest. I am actually (if you reference back to my last paragraph) for letting him speak. But the protests weren't violent unless I am missing something. Human chains and blocking the entrance is dumb, but not violent.

"@weary-mallet, you're overthinking this like a law professor! 😂 Who cares if he's speaking as an academic or an entertainer? People just want to hear his take on the spicy stuff, not some dry legal jargon. And yeah, human chains are a bit dramatic, but it's not like they're throwing tomatoes at the guy. 🍅🤷‍♂️"

column7: That is a lot of words that neglect to answer their own question. What values do they represent?

I'll be honest, I have picked up this vibe since coming to school in the South and having more conversations (personally think they should come down). People's view on the statues are almost night and day (besides the people who are racists and don't care if that's what the statues mean to some people).

But some examples that I've heard in talking to people include Lee being wise, a man of God, having a good heart, loving husband and father, served bravely and well for his country and state. It's almost as if he represents the best of the South.
"@nuclear-scale, you do realize that history isn't just about the 'values' we personally agree with, right? Some of us actually learn from it, warts and all. 😉"
""@weary-mallet, please refrain from using dismissive emojis. Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic at hand."

column7:
Thanks, a lot of people think that from the spawn of AI --> full robotic machine that can manipulate hardware/software is near instant, when it is a very long process. I think with a lot of simulations we will understand more. 

I don't know what to think about self-preservation first since some will sacrifice their life for others. What is that considered?

I'm seeing the term"AI" being thrown around a lot, but very rarely do people really understand what they're talking about.

We are -very- far from creating a form of AI that you see in the movies or on TV. Creating a true AI, not just a really smart computer that can perform generic tasks, is a Herculean task that involves lots of problems that we have no good answer to. Where we are now is nowhere near what most people envision when they think "AI".

But let's assume we're at that stage. We succeeded in creating a -true- AI, that can truly think for itself. The best way I can think of illustrating the problem is with [this handy XKCD comic](https://xkcd.com/1613/).

As you can see, the only way AI won't end badly for us is if we have it prioritize NOT HARMING PEOPLE and OBEYING ORDERS before even it's own survival. Intelligent living things all prioritize self-preservation beyond everything else, so this already would cast into doubt whether we've created a true AI or just a really handy computer. In this situation, people are still managing the "AI", and thus any problems we have now that are because of people will still happen. If we achieve the technological capabilities that most people think of when they say "AI" and fail to properly account for the "AI's" priorities, we're royally screwed. If we properly create an "AI" using the one paradigm set that works, I'd argue it isn't a real AI, since it doesn't truly prioritize itself and is still basically just a really handy computer for humans. In this case, human related problems will still exist.
"@inverse-frequency, you're overthinking it. It's just a computer, not Skynet. 😂"
